Package com.acs.smartcardio

package com.acs.smartcardio
Provides classes and interfaces for communicating with ACS Bluetooth readers on Android 4.3 (Jelly Bean MR2) or above.

Declaring BLE Permissions

In order to use Bluetooth features in your application, you must declare the following permissions in your application manifest file. 

 <uses-permission
     android:name="android.permission.BLUETOOTH"
     android:maxSdkVersion="30" />

 <uses-permission
     android:name="android.permission.BLUETOOTH_ADMIN"
     android:maxSdkVersion="30" />

 <uses-permission
     android:name="android.permission.BLUETOOTH_SCAN"
     android:usesPermissionFlags="neverForLocation" />

 <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.BLUETOOTH_CONNECT" />

 <uses-permission
     android:name="android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION"
     android:maxSdkVersion="30" />

 <uses-permission
     android:name="android.permission.ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION"
     android:maxSdkVersion="28" />

Targeting Android 12 or later

If you want to target your app to Android 12 or later, you need to declare BLUETOOTH_SCAN and BLUETOOTH_CONNECT permission.

Targeting Android 11 or earlier

If you want to target your app to Android 11 or earlier, you need to declare BLUETOOTH, BLUETOOTH_ADMIN and ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION permission. If you want to target your app to Android 9 or earlier, you can declare ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION permission instead of ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION permission.

Checking BLE feature

If your app is designed to run on BLE-capable devices only, you will need to include the following in your app's manifest. 

 <uses-feature
     android:name="android.hardware.bluetooth_le"
     android:required="true" />

However, you can set required="false" and check BLE feature at run-time using PackageManager.hasSystemFeature(). 

 // Use this check to determine whether BLE is supported on the device. Then
 // you can selectively disable BLE-related features.
 if (!getPackageManager().hasSystemFeature(PackageManager.FEATURE_BLUETOOTH_LE)) {

     Toast.makeText(this, R.string.ble_not_supported, Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
     finish();
 }

Setting Up BLE

If BLE is supported on the device, you will need to enable Bluetooth in order to connect card terminals. To enable Bluetooth, you need to get the instance of BluetoothAdapter from BluetoothManager. 

 private BluetoothAdapter mBluetoothAdapter;

 ...

 // Initializes Bluetooth adapter.
 final BluetoothManager bluetoothManager =
         (BluetoothManager) getSystemService(Context.BLUETOOTH_SERVICE);
 mBluetoothAdapter = bluetoothManager.getAdapter();

To check whether Bluetooth is enabled or not, call isEnabled() from BluetoothAdapter. Then, call ActivityResultLauncher.launch() to request user permission to enable Bluetooth.

For Android 12 or later, you must request BLUETOOTH_CONNECT permission at run-time in order to enable Bluetooth. 

 private final ActivityResultLauncher<Intent> mEnableBtLauncher =
         registerForActivityResult(
                 new ActivityResultContracts.StartActivityForResult(), result -> {

                     // User chose not to enable Bluetooth.
                     if (result.getResultCode() == Activity.RESULT_CANCELED) {
                         finish();
                     }
                 });

 private final ActivityResultLauncher<String> mEnableBtPermissionLauncher =
         registerForActivityResult(
                 new ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission(), isGranted -> {

                     if (isGranted) {

                         Intent intent = new Intent(BluetoothAdapter.ACTION_REQUEST_ENABLE);
                         mEnableBtLauncher.launch(intent);
                     }
                 });

 ...

 // Ensures Bluetooth is enabled on the device.  If Bluetooth is not currently enabled,
 // fire an intent to display a dialog asking the user to grant permission to enable it.
 if (!mBluetoothAdapter.isEnabled()) {

     // Android 12 or later requires BLUETOOTH_CONNECT permission to enable Bluetooth.
     if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.S) {

         if (ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission(this, Manifest.permission.BLUETOOTH_CONNECT)
                 == PackageManager.PERMISSION_GRANTED) {

             Intent intent = new Intent(BluetoothAdapter.ACTION_REQUEST_ENABLE);
             mEnableBtLauncher.launch(intent);

         } else {

             mEnableBtPermissionLauncher.launch(Manifest.permission.BLUETOOTH_CONNECT);
         }

     } else {

         Intent intent = new Intent(BluetoothAdapter.ACTION_REQUEST_ENABLE);
         mEnableBtLauncher.launch(intent);
     }
 }

Initializing Java Smart Card I/O API

To use Java Smart Card I/O API with BLE card terminals, you need to call getInstance() from BluetoothSmartCard. This class is a singleton and takes a Context as a parameter. If you call this method within Activity, you can pass this to the parameter. 

 private BluetoothTerminalManager mManager;
 private TerminalFactory mFactory;

 ...

 // Get the Bluetooth terminal manager.
 mManager = BluetoothSmartCard.getInstance(this).getManager();
 if (mManager == null) {

     Toast.makeText(this, R.string.error_bluetooth_not_supported, Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
     finish();
     return;
 }

 // Get the terminal factory.
 mFactory = BluetoothSmartCard.getInstance(this).getFactory();
 if (mFactory == null) {

     Toast.makeText(this, R.string.error_bluetooth_provider_not_found,
             Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
     finish();
     return;
 }

Finding BLE Card Terminals

To find BLE card terminals, you must use startScan() method from BluetoothTerminalManager. You need to supply a terminal type and a callback for returning CardTerminal object. Because continuing to scan drains the battery, you must call stopScan() after your card terminal was found.

For Android 6 or later, you must request the following permissions at run-time in order to scan BLE devices:

Permissions for BLE devices
Operating System Permissions
Android 12 or later BLUETOOTH_SCAN and BLUETOOTH_CONNECT
Android 10 - 11 ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION
Android 6 - 9 ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION
Android 4.3 - 5.1 N/A 
 private Handler mHandler;
 private Button mScanButton;
 private TerminalAdapter mTerminalAdapter;

 ...

 // Initialize Scan button.
 mHandler = new Handler();
 mScanButton = findViewById(R.id.activity_main_button_scan);
 mScanButton.setOnClickListener(v -> {

     if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.S) {

         // Android 12 or later: Request BLUETOOTH_SCAN and BLUETOOTH_CONNECT.
         requestBtScanPermissions(new String[]{Manifest.permission.BLUETOOTH_SCAN,
                 Manifest.permission.BLUETOOTH_CONNECT});

     } else if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.Q) {

         // Android 10 - 11: Request ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION.
         requestBtScanPermissions(new String[]{Manifest.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION});

     } else if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.M) {

         // Android 6 - 9: Request ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION.
         requestBtScanPermissions(new String[]{Manifest.permission.ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION});

     } else {

         // Android 4.3 - 5.1: No need to request the permission.

         // Select a terminal type.
         DialogFragment fragment = new TerminalTypeDialogFragment();
         fragment.show(getSupportFragmentManager(), "terminal_type");
     }
 });

 ...

 private void requestBtScanPermissions(String[] permissions) {

     boolean granted = true;

     // Check if all permissions are granted.
     for (String p : permissions) {

         granted = granted && (ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission(this, p)
                 == PackageManager.PERMISSION_GRANTED);
     }

     if (granted) {

         // Select a terminal type.
         DialogFragment fragment = new TerminalTypeDialogFragment();
         fragment.show(getSupportFragmentManager(), "terminal_type");

     } else {

         mBtScanPermissionLauncher.launch(permissions);
     }
 }

The result will be returned from ActivityResultCallback. After the permission was granted, the app can start the scan. 

 private final ActivityResultLauncher<String[]> mBtScanPermissionLauncher =
         registerForActivityResult(
                 new ActivityResultContracts.RequestMultiplePermissions(), isGranted -> {

                     if (!isGranted.containsValue(false)) {

                         // Select a terminal type.
                         DialogFragment fragment = new TerminalTypeDialogFragment();
                         fragment.show(getSupportFragmentManager(), "terminal_type");
                     }
                 });

 ...

 @Override
 public void onDialogItemClick(DialogFragment dialog, int which) {

     mScanButton.setEnabled(false);
     mTerminalAdapter.clear();

     // Start the scan.
     mManager.startScan(which,
             terminal -> runOnUiThread(() -> mTerminalAdapter.addTerminal(terminal)));

     // Stop the scan.
     mHandler.postDelayed(() -> {

         mManager.stopScan();
         mScanButton.setEnabled(true);
     }, SCAN_PERIOD);
 }

Authenticating a BLE Card Terminal

Normally, the BLE card terminal will use the default key in authentication. If you have modified the master key, you must call setMasterKey() before connecting to a card. 

 byte[] newKey = {
         0x00, 0x01, 0x02, 0x03, 0x04, 0x05, 0x06, 0x08,
         0x00, 0x01, 0x02, 0x03, 0x04, 0x05, 0x06, 0x08
 };

 mManager.setMasterKey(terminal, newKey);

Connecting to a Card

To connect a card, you must call connect() from your CardTerminal object to return a Card object. The available protocols are T=0, T=1, T=0 or T=1 and direct. 

 // Protocol:
 // "T=0"    - T=0
 // "T=1"    - T=1
 // "*"      - T=0 or T=1
 // "direct" - Direct mode
 try {
     Card card = terminal.connect("*");
 } catch (CardException e) {
     e.printStackTrace();
 }

Disconnecting the Card

After using the card, you can disconnect it by calling disconnect() from the Card object. If you want to reset the card after disconnecting, you can pass true to the parameter. 

 try {
     card.disconnect(false);
 } catch (CardException e) {
     e.printStackTrace();
 }

Transmitting APDUs

If the card is connected successfully, you must open a channel to transmit APDUs. You can call either getBasicChannel() or openLogicalChannel() from Card object. After a CardChannel object is returned, you can transmit APDUs using transmit(CommandAPDU) or transmit(ByteBuffer, ByteBuffer) from the CardChannel object. 

 byte[] command = { 0x00, (byte) 0x84, 0x00, 0x00, 0x08 };

 try {

     Card card = terminal.connect("*");
     CardChannel channel = card.getBasicChannel();
     CommandAPDU commandAPDU = new CommandAPDU(command);
     ResponseAPDU responseAPDU = channel.transmit(commandAPDU);

 } catch (CardException e) {

     e.printStackTrace();
 }

These methods issue a GET RESPONSE command using SW2 as the Le field if 61 XX is received and reissue the command using SW2 as the Le field if 6C XX is received. To modify the default behaviour, you can set the option by calling methods from TransmitOptions class. 

 TransmitOptions.setT0GetResponse(false);
 TransmitOptions.setT1GetResponse(false);

Transmitting Control Commands

If the card is connected successfully, you can call transmitControlCommand() from the Card object to transmit control commands. 

 int controlCode = BluetoothTerminalManager.IOCTL_ESCAPE;

 try {

     Card card = terminal.connect("direct");
     byte[] response = card.transmitControlCommand(controlCode, command);

 } catch (CardException e) {

     e.printStackTrace();
 }

Modifying the Timeout Settings

To get the timeout settings of the card terminal, you must call getTimeouts() from the BluetoothTerminalManager object to return TerminalTimeouts object.

You can modify the timeout from the following methods:

  • setConnectionTimeout()
  • setPowerTimeout()
  • setProtocolTimeout()
  • setApduTimeout()
  • setControlTimeout()
 TerminalTimeouts timeouts = mManager.getTimeouts(terminal);
 timeouts.setApduTimeout(20000);

Getting the Battery Status

You can call getBatteryStatus() from the BluetoothTerminalManager object to return the battery status. If the card terminal does not support the battery status, it will return BATTERY_STATUS_NOT_SUPPORTED. 

 try {
     int batteryStatus = mManager.getBatteryStatus(terminal, 10000);
 } catch (CardException e) {
     e.printStackTrace();
 }

Getting the Battery Level

You can call getBatteryLevel() from the BluetoothTerminalManager object to return the battery level. If the card terminal does not support the battery level, it will return -1. 

 try {
     int batteryLevel = mManager.getBatteryLevel(terminal, 10000);
 } catch (CardException e) {
     e.printStackTrace();
 }

Getting the Device Information

You can call getDeviceInfo() from the BluetoothTerminalManager object to return the device information. If the card terminal does not support the device information, it will return null. 

 int type = BluetoothTerminalManager.DEVICE_INFO_MODEL_NUMBER_STRING;

 try {
     String deviceInfo = mManager.getDeviceInfo(terminal, type, 10000);
 } catch (CardException e) {
     e.printStackTrace();
 }

Disconnecting the BLE Card Terminal

The library connects the BLE card terminal automatically when calling connect() from the CardTerminal object. To terminate the Bluetooth connection manually, you must call disconnect() from BluetoothTerminalManager object. 

 mManager.disconnect(terminal);
Since:
0.5
  • Class
    Description
    AcsBluetooth
    The AcsBluetooth class is a Bluetooth provider for TerminalFactory.
    AcsBluetooth.Factory
    The Factory class implements service provider interface for TerminalFactory.
    Amr220cConstants
    The Amr220cConstants class contains constants for AMR220-C.
    BluetoothSmartCard
    The BluetoothSmartCard class is a singleton that provides Bluetooth terminal manager and terminal factory.
    BluetoothTerminalManager
    The BluetoothTerminalManager class manages card terminals and uses as a parameter to initialize ACS Bluetooth provider.
    BluetoothTerminalManager.TerminalScanCallback
    Callback interface used to deliver terminal scan results.
    TerminalTimeouts
    The TerminalTimeouts class defines operation timeouts for card terminals.
    TransmitOptions
    The TransmitOptions class controls options in transmitting APDUs.
